_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for DeKalb County, GA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. DeKalb's courthouse in Decatur survived the Civil War, and records are generally complete from the county's 1822 formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame courthouse replaced by a larger building.
Brick courthouse. Survived the Civil War and Battle of Decatur (1864). Replaced 1898.

record_losses.cause: No major fire or war damage to records
record_losses.description: DeKalb County's courthouse survived the Civil War without significant damage to records, despite the Battle of Decatur (1864) being fought nearby. Records run from the county's 1822 formation with good continuity. Minor wear on early volumes but no catastrophic losses.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in DeKalb County after 1822, the records almost certainly survive. Gaps in the paper trail reflect the ancestor's circumstances rather than courthouse losses.
record_losses.source: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.gaps: No major gaps. Early volumes show wear but are legible.
deeds.repository: DeKalb County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Department of Archives and History microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com GA land records

wills_estates.repository: DeKalb County Probate Court; GA Department of Archives and History

marriages.gaps: Earlier marriages may not have been consistently recorded at the county level.

court_minutes.repository: DeKalb County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Department of Archives and History

substitute_sources.description: DeKalb County was settled under the Georgia Land Lottery system. The 1821 lottery records list drawers and their assigned lots.
substitute_sources.repository: GA Department of Archives and History
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for establishing initial landowners. Even though the courthouse records survive, the lottery records provide the initial land distribution that precedes the deed chain.
substitute_sources.source: Cadle, Farris W. Georgia Land Surveying History and Law. University of Georgia Press, 1991.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1830-1940 (DeKalb first enumerated 1830). Slave schedules 1850-1860. Agricultural schedules 1850-1880.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families between deed records.

substitute_sources.description: The granite quarrying operation at Stone Mountain employed hundreds of workers. Company records — where they survive — document workers' names and employment dates.
substitute_sources.repository: DeKalb History Center; GA Department of Archives and History (fragmentary)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for identifying quarry workers. Quarry employment records can establish residence dates when other records are silent.
substitute_sources.source: Freeman, David B. Carved in Stone: The History of Stone Mountain. Mercer University Press, 1997.

narrative_hooks: DeKalb County's courthouse survived the Battle of Decatur in 1864 and every decade since — {ancestor_name}'s records almost certainly exist if {pronoun_subject} owned property or appeared in court.
Georgia did not require marriage licenses statewide until 1805 (through the county ordinary), so early DeKalb marriages may be recorded as bonds or in church records rather than civil licenses.
The Georgia Land Lottery records show who drew DeKalb County lots in 1821 — the starting point for {ancestor_name}'s land ownership chain.
Georgia birth and death registration began in 1919, so the absence of a vital record for {ancestor_name} before that date is standard — not a mystery.
source_references: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the Georgia State Archives.
Cadle, Farris W. Georgia Land Surveying History and Law. Athens: University of Georgia Press, 1991.
Freeman, David B. Carved in Stone: The History of Stone Mountain. Macon, GA: Mercer University Press, 1997.
